title: Chapter 9 Quiz
description: Prove your knowledge of Protocol-Oriented Programming and Extensions!

questions.question: What specifically is a Protocol in Swift?
options: A chunk of executable code shared across classes.
A strict blueprint or 'contract' that outlines required methods and properties without actually implementing the code.
questions.explanation: A protocol establishes an absolute baseline requirement (e.g. 'Must have an ID') without caring HOW the object achieves it.

questions.question: Why don't we see curly braces `{ }` containing logic inside a normal protocol declaration?
options: Because protocols do not hold execution logic. They only define the function signature (e.g., `func jump()`).
Because protocols only use square brackets.
questions.explanation: The protocol lays down the law. The Struct or Class that ADOPTS the protocol is the one responsible for writing the actual `{ }` logic to obey the law.

questions.question: When declaring that a struct conforms to a protocol, you separate the struct name and the protocol name with a _____.
questions.explanation: The colon `:` syntax indicates inheritance for classes, and protocol adherence for structs and classes alike (e.g., `struct Car: Drivable`).

questions.question: If `protocol Flyer { func takeOff() }`, what happens if you write `struct Bird: Flyer { }` without adding any functions?
options: takeOff runs with a blank default.
Swift throws a compiler error stating 'Bird does not conform to protocol Flyer'.
questions.explanation: Protocol conformance is absolute. If you sign the contract by adding `: Flyer`, Swift forces you to immediately implement the required code.

questions.question: Can a Struct adopt 5 different Protocols simultaneously?
options: Yes, you just separate them with commas.
No, Structs can't use protocols, only Classes can.
questions.explanation: Unlike inheritance (which limits you to 1 parent), you can stack an infinite amount of modular protocols onto a single Struct.

questions.question: What feature allows you to retroactively add brand new functions to Apple's built-in Types (like `Int`, `String`, or `Array`)?
questions.explanation: Extensions act like software patches. You simply type `extension String { ... }` and attach entirely new functionality to every string in the app.

questions.question: Can an Extension be used to add completely new Stored Properties (like `var count: Int = 0`) to an existing Type?
questions.explanation: While an Extension can add Methods and COMPUTED Properties, they absolutely cannot expand the memory footprint by adding new STORED properties. Memory size is locked upon original declaration.

questions.question: What is the ultimate benefit of writing an `extension` ON a Protocol?
options: It allows you to provide a default block of functioning code, so that adopting Types don't have to keep writing the same code over and over.
It makes the app compile faster.
questions.explanation: Protocol Extensions unlock 'Protocol-Oriented Programming'. It provides the free code-sharing of Class Inheritance without all the rigid, dangerous baggage!

questions.question: If a Class is adopting a Protocol AND inheriting from a Superclass simultaneously, what is the syntax order?
questions.explanation: The Superclass MUST always be listed first, followed by a comma and then any adopted Protocols.

questions.question: Protocols can inherit from other Protocols.
questions.explanation: Yes. A protocol like `Combatant` could legally inherit from `Movable`, combining both rulebooks into one massive requirement for any adopter.

questions.question: In the protocol declaration `var id: String { get set }`, what does `{ get set }` mean?
options: The property must be a constant (let).
The property must be readable, but cannot be changed.
The property must be completely readable (get) and modifiable (set) by the outside world.
questions.explanation: Protocols use `{ get }` to demand read-access, or `{ get set }` to demand both read and write access for a property.

questions.question: Which of these is Swift's most recognizable and frequently used protocol inside iOS Development?
questions.explanation: In SwiftUI, absolutely everything is a Struct that adopts the `View` protocol!

questions.question: If you want to create an Array that holds both `Dog` structs and `Cat` structs, you can give them both the `Animal` protocol, and create the array as `var pets: [_____] = []`.
questions.explanation: This is Polymorphism! You can type an Array using a Protocol instead of a concrete object. It accepts anything that perfectly follows the rules.

questions.question: Apple describes Swift as a 'Protocol-Oriented' language. What does this philosophy mean?
options: You must secure all HTTP routes.
Prefer composing small, reusable behaviors using Protocols and Structs rather than building massive, rigid Class inheritance trees.
questions.explanation: POP (Protocol-Oriented Programming) solves the massive scaling problems inherent to Object-Oriented design by keeping data decoupled and modular.

questions.question: If an `Int` is extended to include `func squared() -> Int`, how would you call it on the number 5?
questions.explanation: Because the extension permanently fused the method to the Type itself, you execute it using standard `instance.method()` dot notation!
